title: How parts polarize during relationship conflict
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
When you and your partner fight, it's not really you fighting

htmlContent:
When you and your partner fight, it's not really you fighting

I know that sounds strange. Stay with me here. When I'm in the middle of a conflict with someone I love, it feels like my survival is on the line. My heart races. My chest tightens. Words come out of my mouth that I don't even recognize as mine. And later, I sit alone wondering who that person was. The one who said those things. The one who couldn't stop, couldn't soften, couldn't just let it go.

That person wasn't really me. Not the me I am when I feel safe. That was a part of me. A part that got activated, grabbed the wheel, and drove us both off a cliff.

Here's the thing about parts. They're not flaws. They're not signs that you're broken or bad at relationships. They're survival strategies that got encoded in your nervous system long before this argument, this relationship, this moment. Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems (IFS), describes parts as subpersonalities that develop to protect us from overwhelming experiences. And when conflict hits, those parts don't just show up. They polarize.

Polarization means two parts take opposite positions and lock into a battle neither can win. Think about the last argument you had. Maybe one part of you wanted to fight back, defend yourself, prove you were right. And another part wanted to shut down, disappear, keep the peace at any cost. Those two parts were polarized. They were pulling you in opposite directions, and neither one trusted the other.

That's the thing. Polarization isn't just between you and your partner. It's happening inside you. And until you can see that, the conflict outside will keep mirroring the conflict inside.

The protector parts that show up when you feel threatened

When your nervous system detects threat, it doesn't check to see if the threat is real or imagined. It doesn't ask if this is a safe disagreement or a dangerous attack. It just reacts. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us that our nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety and danger. And when it detects danger, it mobilizes us for fight, flight, freeze, or fawn.

Your parts are doing the same thing. They're scanning the relational environment. And when they sense conflict, they activate to protect you. But here's the problem. They're working with old data. Data from when you were small, helpless, and completely dependent on caregivers who may have been unpredictable, dismissive, or outright dangerous.

Let me give you an example. There's a part of me that becomes a prosecutor during arguments. It's sharp, articulate, and relentless. It gathers evidence. It builds a case. It waits for the perfect moment to deliver the closing argument. That part believes if I can prove I'm right, I'll be safe. If I can win the argument, I'll be seen. If I can make my partner wrong, I'll finally be right.

But that part doesn't see the cost. It doesn't see my partner shrinking. It doesn't see the distance growing between us. It only sees the threat and the mission to neutralize it.

And then there's the other part. The one that shows up after the fight. The one that's flooded with shame and regret. The one that says, "See? You always ruin everything. You're too much. You're too intense. No one can love you like this." That part is also a protector. It's trying to keep me small so I don't get rejected. It's trying to preemptively punish me so I'll learn to stay quiet next time.

These two parts are polarized. The prosecutor part thinks the shame part is weak and dangerous. It says, "If I let that part take over, I'll disappear. I'll become a doormat. I'll lose myself." And the shame part thinks the prosecutor part is reckless and destructive. It says, "If I let that part run the show, I'll destroy every relationship I have." So they fight. And I get stuck in the middle, torn between fighting and collapsing, never able to find the middle ground.

This is what polarization looks like in real time. And it's exhausting. Right?

How your partner's parts trigger your parts and vice versa

Here's where it gets really interesting. And painful. Your parts don't just react to your partner. They react to your partner's parts. And your partner's parts react to yours. It's a feedback loop that can spin out of control in seconds.

Let me paint a picture. Say your partner has a part that feels abandoned easily. Maybe they grew up with a parent who was emotionally unavailable. That part is hypervigilant. It scans for signs of withdrawal, distance, rejection. When you need space after a disagreement, that part doesn't see a healthy boundary. It sees confirmation of its deepest fear: "I'm being left. I'm not important. I'm going to be alone."

So that part activates. It might get clingy, demanding, or accusatory. It might say, "You always pull away. You don't care about me. You're just going to leave like everyone else."

Now your parts react. If you have a part that feels smothered or controlled, it sees that clinginess as a threat. It says, "I need space to breathe. This is too much. I'm being trapped." So it pushes harder. It withdraws further. It gets colder.

And your partner's abandoned part sees that coldness and panics. It escalates. It pursues harder. It says more desperate things. And your smothered part escalates right back. It withdraws more. It shuts down completely.

This is polarization between two people. Your parts are dancing with each other's parts. And neither of you is actually present. Neither of you is the adult in the room. You're both being run by protector parts that are fighting an old war.

Pete Walker, who wrote extensively about complex PTSD and its relational impacts, called this the "outer critic" and "inner critic" dynamic. The outer critic sees the partner as the threat. The inner critic turns that threat inward. And the result is a painful loop where both people feel attacked, misunderstood, and alone.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Walker breaks down exactly how these relational patterns form and what it takes to start untangling them. If you've ever wondered why your reactions feel so much bigger than the situation warrants, that book will help you understand.

The exile underneath the protector

Here's the thing about protectors. They're not the real problem. They're doing a job. A hard, thankless job. They're standing guard over something much more vulnerable. Something that got hurt a long time ago and never got healed.

In IFS, these vulnerable parts are called exiles. They carry the raw emotions from childhood wounds. Shame. Terror. Grief. Helplessness. Loneliness. The protector parts keep those exiles locked away because they believe if those feelings ever got out, you wouldn't survive. You'd fall apart. You'd be annihilated.

So when conflict triggers a protector, it's not really about the argument. It's about keeping the exile safe. The prosecutor part isn't fighting to win. It's fighting to keep you from feeling the shame of being unseen, unheard, unimportant. The shame part isn't trying to punish you. It's trying to keep you small enough to stay safe, to avoid the rejection that felt like death when you were a child.

This is why arguments can feel so intense. It's not about who left the dishes in the sink or who forgot to send that text. It's about survival. Your nervous system doesn't know the difference between a disagreement with your partner and a life-threatening abandonment. It just knows danger. And it activates the same protective responses it used when you were five years old.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. She teaches a way of meeting these exiled parts with compassion instead of more rejection. And honestly, that's what most of us need. Not more strategies for winning arguments. But more capacity to hold our own pain without making it someone else's fault.

Think about it. When you're in a fight, can you pause for one second and ask yourself: "What part of me is activated right now? And what is it afraid would happen if it stopped protecting me?"

That question alone can shift the entire dynamic. Not because it fixes everything. But because it brings you back into your body. It reminds you that you're not just a bundle of reactive parts. There's a Self underneath all of them. A Self that can witness, hold, and eventually heal.

What to do when you're both triggered and nothing is working

I'm not going to give you a five-step plan that will make all your conflicts disappear. Because that's not how this works. Polarization doesn't resolve through technique. It resolves through presence. Through the slow, patient work of learning to stay in your body when everything in you wants to fight or flee.

But there are some things that help. Here's what I've learned from my own practice and from teachers like Janina Fisher and Deb Dana, who both work at the intersection of trauma and the nervous system.

First, recognize when you're blended with a part. Blending means you've become the part. You're not aware of it as a part of you. You just think that's who you are. "I am angry." "I am hurt." "I am done." When you're blended, you have no choice. You're on autopilot. The first step is to notice. Just notice. "Oh, there's a part of me that's really angry right now. I wonder what it's afraid of."

Second, slow down. Literally. Take a breath. Feel your feet on the floor. Put your hand on your chest. This isn't woo-woo. This is nervous system regulation. When you slow down, you send a signal to your brain that you're not in immediate danger. And that signal gives your parts a chance to relax, even a little.

Third, get curious instead of defensive. This is hard. Really hard. But you can ask your partner, "What part of you is activated right now? What does it need?" Or you can say, "I notice a part of me wants to defend myself. Can we pause for a minute?"

Fourth, take a break if you need to. Not a silent treatment. Not a cold withdrawal. A deliberate, communicated pause. "I'm feeling flooded. I need twenty minutes to regulate. I'm not leaving you. I'm coming back." Then actually come back.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay Gibson. It helps explain why certain relational triggers feel so intense. Because if you grew up with parents who couldn't handle your emotions, you learned to suppress parts of yourself. And those suppressed parts will keep showing up in your adult relationships until they're finally seen and held.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. It's not a light read. But it gives you the framework for understanding why your nervous system responds the way it does. And that understanding, in itself, can be healing.

Here's what I want you to take away from this. Polarization is not a sign that your relationship is broken. It's a sign that your parts are doing their jobs. They're trying to protect you. They're trying to keep you safe. And they're working with the tools they have.

The work isn't to get rid of your parts. It's to build enough trust with them that they can step back and let you lead. It's to let them know that you're an adult now. You have resources. You have choices. You're not the helpless child anymore.

That takes time. It takes practice. And it takes a lot of compassion for the parts that still believe the old story.

So the next time you find yourself in a fight, notice what happens inside you. Notice the part that wants to strike. The part that wants to flee. The part that wants to apologize even though you haven't done anything wrong. They're all there. They're all trying to help.

You don't have to let any of them drive. You can just sit in the driver's seat, hands on the wheel, and say, "I see you. I hear you. I've got this."

And maybe, over time, your parts will start to believe you.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's guide for C-PTSD survivors
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - Validates childhood experiences and relational patterns
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - Judith Herman's foundational text on trauma recovery
